LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFF JANE DOE'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, MOTION FOR RECONSIDERATION OF THE SEPTEMBER 15, 2017 ORDER GRANTING SUMMARY JUDGMENT IN FAVOR OF DEFENDANTS, OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE, FOR RELIEF UNDER CCP SECTION 473 IS TREATED AS AN INVITATION BY MS. DOE TO THE COURT TO EXERCISE ITS DISCRETION PER LE FRANCOIS V. GOEL (2005) 35 CAL. 4TH 1094 TO RECONSIDER THE SEPTEMBER 15 ORDER WITHOUT A SHOWING THAT THE REQUIREMENTS OF CCP 1008 HAVE BEEN ESTABLISHED AND, AS SO TREATED, THE COURT ACCEPTS THAT INVITATION. BECAUSE DEFENDANTS DID NOT SUBSTANTIVELY RESPOND TO THE ARGUMENTS MADE BY MS. DOE ON THIS MOTION, THE MOTION IS CONTINUED TO DECEMBER 12, 2017 AT 9:30 AM IN DEPARTMENT 302/LAW AND MOTION TO GIVE DEFENDANTS AN OPPORTUNITY TO DO SO. DEFENDANTS MAY FILE SUPPLEMENTAL OPPOSITION PAPERS NO LATER THAN NOVEMBER 28, 2017 AND MS. DOE MAY FILE SUPPLEMENTAL REPLY PAPERS NO LATER THAN DECEMBER 5, 2017. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN: J. RIOS MERIDA; REPORTER: REYES F. HUNTER, CSR NO. 6576, E-MAIL: RHUNTER@SFTC.ORG

LAW AND MOTION 302,DEFENDANT THE PERMANENTE MEDICAL GROUP, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION ON PLAINTIFF JANE DOE'S REQUEST FOR PUNITIVE DAMAGES IS GRANTED. THE UNDISPUTED FACTS SHOW THAT DIANE EASTERWOOD, WAS NOT AN EMPLOYEE OF TPMG. (EASTERWOOD DEC. 2; EASTERWOOD DEPO. P. 9). UNLIKE DAVIS V. KIEWIT PACIFIC CO. (2013) 220 CAL.APP.4TH 358, 371 WHERE THE PERSON IN CHARGE OF INVESTIGATIONS UNDER THE COMPANY HR POLICY WAS AN EMPLOYEE OF THE DEFENDANT, MS. EASTERWOOD WAS NOT AN EMPLOYEE, DIRECTOR, OFFICER OR MANAGING AGENT OF TPMG. ALTHOUGH PLAINTIFF HAS ADDUCED EVIDENCE OF RETALIATORY BEHAVIOR BY HER SUPERVISOR MS. YOUNG, THAT EVIDENCE DOES NOT CREATE A TRIABLE ISSUE WHETHER MS. YOUNG WAS A MANAGING AGENT. BECAUSE THERE IS NO EVIDENCE THAT TPMG ITSELF ENGAGED IN ACTS OF MALICE, FRAUD, OR OPPRESSION AGAINST PLAINTIFF, NOR THAT ANY OF ITS OFFICERS, DIRECTORS OR MANAGING AGENTS RATIFIED, AUTHORIZED OR CONSCIOUSLY DISREGARDED SUCH ACTS, TPMG IS ENTITLED TO SUMMARY ADJUDICATION IN ITS FAVOR ON PLAINTIFF'S REQUEST FOR PUNITIVE DAMAGES. PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE A FORM OF ORDER AND TO COMPLY WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE; HAROLD KAHN; CLERK: CYNTHIA HERBERT; NOT REPORTED. =302/HK

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANTS DIANE EASTERWOOD, LAWANDA YOUNG, THE PERMANENTE MEDICAL GROUP, INC. AND KAISER FOUNDATION HOSPITALS' MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. THE CAUSE IS ARGUED AND THE COURT RULES AS FOLLOWS: GRANTED IN FAVOR OF ALL FOUR MOVING DEFENDANTS AND AGAINST PLAINTIFF JANE DOE. THE OCTOBER 16, 2017 TRIAL DATE IS VACATED. ENTRY OF JUDGMENT IS DELAYED UNTIL OCTOBER 17 OR AFTER. PLAINTIFF IS TO FILE A MOTION FOR LEAVE TO AMEND NO LATER THAN OCTOBER 16, 2017 AND MUST RESERVE A HEARING DATE. PLAINTIFF'S FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT, HER OPERATIVE PLEADING, ALLEGES FIVE CAUSES OF ACTION. HER OPPOSITION PAPERS TO THIS MOTION STATE THAT SHE WILL DISMISS HER SECOND THROUGH FOURTH CAUSES OF ACTION, THOUGH THOSE CLAIMS HAVE NOT YET BEEN DISMISSED. PLAINTIFF'S STATEMENT IS A CONCESSION THAT THOSE CLAIMS LACK MERIT OR, AT THE LEAST, THAT SHE WILL NOT CONTINUE TO PROSECUTE THEM. DEFENDANTS HAVE SHOWN THAT PLAINTIFF DID NOT EXHAUST HER ADMINISTRATIVE REMEDIES BY FILING A TIMELY CLAIM WITH EITHER THE DFEH OR EEOC AS TO HER TWO REMAINING CLAIMS. MOREOVER, DEFENDANTS HAVE SHOWN THAT DEFENDANT KAISER FOUNDATION HOSPITALS WAS NEVER THE EMPLOYER OF PLAINTIFF, SO ALL CLAIMS AGAINST IT LACK MERIT. DEFENDANTS HAVE FURTHER SHOWN THAT THE TWO INDIVIDUAL DEFENDANTS CANNOT BE LIABLE FOR EITHER THE FIRST CAUSE OF ACTION OR THE PORTION OF THE FIFTH CAUSE OF ACTION ALLEGING DISCRIMINATION. WHILE IT APPEARS THAT DEFENDANTS MAY BE ENTITLED TO JUDGMENT IN THEIR FAVOR ON SOME OR ALL OF THE SUBSTANTIVE FACTUAL ISSUES THEY RAISED IN THEIR MOVING PAPERS, THE COURT DOES NOT AND NEED NOT REACH ANY OF THOSE ISSUES. COUNSEL FOR DEFENDANTS SHALL SUBMIT A [PROPOSED] WRITTEN ORDER THAT TRACKS THE COURT'S RULING, VERBATIM AND THAT COMPLIES WITH CRC 3.1312. THE ORDER SHALL INCLUDE IN TITLE AND BODY THE VACATING OF THE TRIAL DATE. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN; CLERK: J. RIOS MERIDA; REPORTER: ANGIE DINER, CSR NO: 9581, EMAIL: ADINER@SFTC.ORG

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
Off Calendar Apr-03-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT THE PERMANENTE MEDICAL GROUP, INC.'s Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings

The hearing will be at 9am, not 9:30am. Defendant The Permanente Medical Group. Inc.'s motion for judgment on the pleadings is denied. TPMG's standing argument is no longer tenable in light of plaintiff Jane Doe's application to reopen her chapter 7 bankruptcy case; the decision by the appointed bankruptcy trustee for that case not to pursue Ms. Doe's litigation claims against TPMG; and thereafter the bankruptcy court's order closing the reopened case. At the hearing on March 12, 2019 counsel for TPMG stated that TPMG had three different arguments why its motion should be granted based on judicial estoppel or related grounds. Those arguments are that Ms. Doe's claims fail as a matter of law because: 1) the initial chapter 7 bankruptcy papers that were filed in 2014 did not make any reference to Ms. Doe's claims against TPMG; 2) the papers filed in 2018 to reopen the chapter 7 bankruptcy case did not refer to this litigation but only to an EEOC charge that Ms. Doe filed against TPMG; and 3) the papers filed in 2018 to reopen the chapter 7 bankruptcy case state that Ms. Doe did not believe a bankruptcy trustee should be appointed. On this motion for judgment on the pleadings all three of TPMG's arguments lack merit. (Cloud v. Northrop Grumman Corp. (1998) 67 Cal. App. 4th 995, 1011-1021). Per Cloud, which remains good law and has been cited approvingly by numerous subsequent published California decisions, the failure to include a litigation claim other than a claim against a creditor in the bankruptcy case in papers with the bankruptcy court is not grounds to preclude the litigation claim absent evidence that the bankruptcy debtor engaged in a deliberate scheme to mislead and gain an unfair advantage. Since this is a non-evidentiary motion, the requisite factual findings can't be made on this motion. While the precise holding of Cloud defeats TPMG's first argument, the reasoning of Cloud also makes clear that TPMG's second and third arguments are not amenable to resolution in TPMG's favor on this motion. There is no basis on this motion to find that Ms. Doe's 2018 bankruptcy papers failed to refer to this litigation or stated that a bankruptcy trustee should not be appointed because of any effort on Ms. Doe's part to mislead the bankruptcy court or bankruptcy trustee and to gain an unfair advantage. Moreover, judicial estoppel could not apply to Ms. Doe's statement that a bankruptcy trustee should not be appointed because Ms. Doe was not successful in her apparent effort not to have a bankruptcy trustee appointed in her reopened chapter 7 case. (See Gottlieb v. Kest (2006) 141 Cal. App. 4th 110, 137-38 (judicial estoppel does not apply to a position taken by bankruptcy debtor in the bankruptcy case unless the bankruptcy debtor was successful in asserting that position in the bankruptcy case)).

Order is signed

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/HEK)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
Off Calendar Jun-08-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling.
Defendant The Permanente Medical Group, Inc.'s motion for summary adjudication on plaintiff Jane Doe's request for punitive damages is granted. The undisputed facts show that Diane Easterwood, was not an employee of TPMG. (Easterwood Dec. 2; Easterwood Depo. p. 9). Unlike Davis v. Kiewit Pacific Co. (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 358, 371 where the person in charge of investigations under the company HR policy was an employee of the defendant, Ms. Easterwood was not an employee, director, officer or managing agent of TPMG. Although plaintiff has adduced evidence of retaliatory behavior by her supervisor Ms. Young, that evidence does not create a triable issue whether Ms. Young was a managing agent. Because there is no evidence that TPMG itself engaged in acts of malice, fraud, or oppression against plaintiff, nor that any of its officers, directors or managing agents ratified, authorized or consciously disregarded such acts, TPMG is entitled to summary adjudication in its favor on plaintiff's request for punitive damages. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order and to comply with CRC 3.1312. Judge; Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Not reported. =302/hk

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Sep-14-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Laurel A. Mousseau of the law offices of Laurel A. Mousseau (415-987-0692) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Gabriel Neil Rubin of the law offices of Kaufman Dolowich & Voluck LLP (415-926-7637) appears as counsel for defendant/moving party

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling with modification.

DEFENDANTS DIANE EASTERWOOD, LAWANDA YOUNG, THE PERMANENTE MEDICAL GROUP, INC. and KAISER FOUNDATION HOSPITALS' MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication is granted in favor of all four moving defendants and against plaintiff Jane Doe. The October 16, 2017 trial date is vacated. Entry of judgment is delayed until October 17 or after. Plaintiff is to file a motion for leave to amend no later than October 16, 2017 and must reserve a hearing date. Plaintiff's first amended complaint, her operative pleading, alleges five causes of action. Her opposition papers to this motion state that she will dismiss her second through fourth causes of action, though those claims have not yet been dismissed. Plaintiff's statement is a concession that those claims lack merit or, at the least, that she will not continue to prosecute them. Defendants have shown that plaintiff did not exhaust her administrative remedies by filing a timely claim with either the DFEH or EEOC as to her two remaining claims. Moreover, defendants have shown that defendant Kaiser Foundation Hospitals was never the employer of plaintiff, so all claims against it lack merit. Defendants have further shown that the two individual defendants cannot be liable for either the first cause of action or the portion of the fifth cause of action alleging discrimination. While it appears that defendants may be entitled to judgment in their favor on some or all of the substantive factual issues they raised in their moving papers, the court does not and need not reach any of those issues. Counsel for defendants shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312. The order shall include in title and body the vacating of the trial date.
